content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

October 22, 1997

Rita Valenzuela
1514 Ridge Crest Way
Monterey Park, CA 91754

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-481

Dear Ms. Valenzuela:

This is in response to your request for advice under the provisions of the Political Reform
Act.¹

FACTS

You were reelected to office in March of 1997. During your campaign, you made a
personal loan to your committee of $5,000. After the election, the committee had only a small
amount of money remaining. Rather than use those funds to make a partial payment on the
personal loan, you are opting to use those remaining funds for any expenses you will incur in the
performance of your office.

QUESTION AND CONCLUSION

Does the surplus funds rule prevent you from using these excess funds for officeholder
expenses?

No. You may use these remaining funds for officeholder related expenses after you have
forgiven the personal loan you made to the committee. Only funds in excess of expenses, up to
$10,000, may be deposited into an account to be used for officeholder expenses. Any remaining
funds must be distributed to any political party, returned to contributors on a pro rata basis, or
turned over to the State's General Fund.

¹Government Code Section 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-18995,
of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-97-481
Page 2

ANALYSIS

Section 89519 provides that:

Any funds in excess of expenses incurred for the campaign shall be
deemed to be surplus campaign funds and shall be distributed within 90 days after
withdrawal, defeat, or election to office in the following manner:

(a) No more than ten thousand dollars ($10,000) may be deposited in the
candidate's officeholder account;

(b) Any remaining surplus funds shall be distributed to any political party,
returned to contributors on a pro rata basis, or turned over to the General Fund.²

You indicated that rather than repay a portion of the personal loan you made to the
committee, you are opting to use your remaining funds for expenses you will incur in the
performance of your office. Using funds in this manner is appropriate provided all expenses of
the committee have been liquidated. Since your committee still has debt to you, the remaining
funds have yet to become surplus.

Since you are the source of the personal loan to the committee, you may forgive the
payment of the balance of the loan. This will recharacterize the personal loan from you as a
contribution from you. Once this has occurred, any funds remaining in the campaign bank
account become surplus and you may then use up to $10,000 of surplus funds for expenses you
will incur in the performance of your office.³ You may utilize your existing campaign bank
account as your officeholder bank account provided the campaign committee has no other debt.

To accomplish this you would forgive the balance of the loan on Schedule B, Part II of
your Form 490 and report this amount as a contribution from you on Schedule A on your next
semi-annual statement. You would then amend your existing Form 410, Statement of
Organization to reflect that your campaign committee is being converted to your officeholder
committee (i.e., you must include the words "officeholder account" in the name of the
committee). At the same time you should file a Form 501 terminating your candidate intention,
and a Form 502 terminating your campaign bank account. The amended Form 410, the Form
501 and Form 502 must be filed with the Secretary of State. A copy of the amended Form 410
would be filed with your filing officer.

²We interpret the General Fund referenced in Section 89519(b) and 85313(c) to mean the General Fund of
the State of California. (Jones Advice Letter, I-97-084.)

³Funds in the officeholder account are utilized for expenses related to assisting, serving, or communicating
with constituents, or with carrying out the official duties of the elected officer. Expenditures from an officeholder
account may not be made in connection with any campaign for elective office or ballot measure. (Section 85313.)

File No. A-97-481
Page 3

I trust this answers your question sufficiently. If you have additional questions or need to
discuss this, please feel free to contact me at (916)322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division
